reasoning: The PKGBUILD is a standard binary package that downloads pre-built Nylas CLI release tarballs from the official github.com/nylas/cli releases with pinned SHA256 checksums (not SKIP). The package() function only installs the binary and shell completions to standard locations. There are no .install hooks, no pkgver() function, no top-level command execution, and no build/prepare/check functions that execute downloaded code. The deepseek model's 'inconclusive' verdict was caused by an infrastructure error ('[Errno 122] Disk quota exceeded'), not by any actual security finding — every file it actually reviewed (PKGBUILD, completions, docs) returned SAFE. The qwen model correctly identified it as safe after reviewing the PKGBUILD and finding no additional files warranting review (the docs/completions are not referenced by any PKGBUILD function).
disagreement_analysis: The disagreement is not substantive. deepseek-v4-flash reviewed many files (all SAFE) but its final result was forced to 'inconclusive' purely due to a disk quota exceeded error during execution — an environmental/infrastructure failure, not a security concern. qwen3 correctly concluded 'safe' after a lean review. Both models agreed on every file they actually assessed.
coverage_issues: qwen3 reviewed only the PKGBUILD and declined to review the completion scripts; while these are not executed during makepkg, deepseek's review of nylas.bash/zsh/fish (containing eval) confirms they are benign Cobra-generated completions — a thorough audit ideally confirms shell completion files even though they are not build-executed.
learnings: Infrastructure errors (e.g., 'Disk quota exceeded') should be distinguished from genuine security inconclusiveness; the tool should classify environmental failures separately so they don't trigger false disagreement-based re-reviews.
When a model reviews N files all returning SAFE and only fails on a non-security I/O error, the aggregation logic should weight the completed file verdicts rather than collapsing to 'inconclusive'.

For binary AUR packages with pinned checksums and no install hooks/build functions, the in-tree docs and shell completions are out-of-scope for makepkg execution; the leaner qwen approach was appropriate and shows file-selection prioritization (PKGBUILD + .install hooks first) is the correct heuristic.

Completion scripts containing 'eval' reliably trigger scrutiny but are standard Cobra-generated patterns invoking the package's own binary — models handled this correctly here, but it's a recurring near-false-positive worth documenting.
